# UC-11.3.6 · Toll Fraud Detection (Cisco CUCM)

## Description

Toll fraud causes significant financial loss. International premium-rate calls from compromised systems can cost thousands per hour.

## Value

Toll fraud causes significant financial loss. International premium-rate calls from compromised systems can cost thousands per hour.

## Implementation

Monitor CDR for international calls, premium-rate numbers (900, 976), and calls outside business hours. Baseline normal international calling patterns. Alert on anomalous patterns. Block suspicious numbers in real-time.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: UCM CDR analysis.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CDR records (called party number, duration, time of day).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor CDR for international calls, premium-rate numbers (900, 976), and calls outside business hours. Baseline normal international calling patterns. Alert on anomalous patterns. Block suspicious numbers in real-time.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=voip sourcetype="cisco:ucm:cdr"
| where match(calledPartyNumber,"^011|^00") AND duration > 60
| stats count, sum(duration) as total_min by callingPartyNumber, calledPartyNumber
| where count > 10
| sort -total_min
```

Understanding this SPL

**Toll Fraud Detection (Cisco CUCM)** — Toll fraud causes significant financial loss. International premium-rate calls from compromised systems can cost thousands per hour.

Documented **Data sources**: CDR records (called party number, duration, time of day). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): UCM CDR analysis. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: voip; **sourcetype**: cisco:ucm:cdr. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=voip, sourcetype="cisco:ucm:cdr". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(calledPartyNumber,"^011|^00") AND duration > 60` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by callingPartyNumber, calledPartyNumber** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count > 10` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with CUCM RTMT, CallManager service parameters, and CDR/CMR or quality reports in Cisco unified communications admin for the same cluster and interval.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (suspicious calls), Bar chart (international calls by destination), Timeline (unusual calling activity), Geo map (call destinations).

## SPL

```spl
index=voip sourcetype="cisco:ucm:cdr"
| where match(calledPartyNumber,"^011|^00") AND duration > 60
| stats count, sum(duration) as total_min by callingPartyNumber, calledPartyNumber
| where count > 10
| sort -total_min
```

## Visualization

Table (suspicious calls), Bar chart (international calls by destination), Timeline (unusual calling activity), Geo map (call destinations).
